caveats: a: OEWS reports occupations and headcount rather than wage-weighted tasks or current AI exposure.
a: The supplied compensation-to-receipts input exceeds one after vintage and harmonization adjustments, so translating task exposure into economic opportunity warrants extra caution.
rho: The adoption source covers all U.S. businesses, not NAICS 624120.
rho: The five-year value is a projection and depends on state-specific privacy, labor, and Medicaid rules.
e: No direct denominator of firms satisfying all size, independence, recurrence, and transferability criteria was found.
e: The frozen firm count is itself margin-bridged with an estimated margin for a thin-margin, publicly funded industry.
s5: Gallup covers all employer businesses and measures intentions rather than completed transfers.
s5: BizBuySell's home health category mixes medical providers outside NAICS 624120 with nonmedical home-care agencies and is a voluntary marketplace sample.
q: The Medicaid payer statistic uses a broader spending category than NAICS 624120.
q: The federal compensation requirement has exceptions, a future applicability date, and uncertain state implementation.
d5: BLS projects occupation employment across multiple industries, not constant-price output for NAICS 624120.
d5: Employment growth may understate unmet demand while overstating output growth if staffing per client rises.
o: The occupation source combines home health aides with personal care aides and spans multiple industries.
o: The estimate distinguishes substitution for the agency model from software assistance within an agency-delivered service.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Services are commonly delivered through recurring caregiver hours, authorized service plans, day-program attendance, or ongoing support engagements. Revenue may come from Medicaid fee schedules or managed-care contracts, other public programs, grants, long-term-care insurance, or private-pay clients and families.
lens.excluded_activities: Residential care facilities, skilled in-home health care, vocational rehabilitation, captive internal care units, non-control financing vehicles, and informal or unpaid family caregiving.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market providers of recurring or repeat nonresidential social-assistance services for elderly people and people with intellectual, developmental, or physical disabilities, including nonmedical home care, homemaker services, adult day services, companionship, social activities, and group support supplied to external clients or funding agencies.

a.bridge: BLS measures the six-digit industry's occupational headcount rather than wage-weighted AI task exposure: 84.23% of jobs are healthcare-support occupations, including 82.28% home health and personal care aides, while management is 1.77%, business and financial operations 0.96%, community and social service 3.65%, and office support 2.75%. I map the administrative, documentation, scheduling, communication, supervision, intake, billing, and care-plan components within and outside aide roles to exposed current tasks, weighting higher-paid office and managerial roles more heavily than headcount. This may understate exposure because aides also document, coordinate, and communicate, but may overstate substitution because those tasks are interwoven with regulated, safety-sensitive human care.
caveats: OEWS reports occupations and headcount rather than wage-weighted tasks or current AI exposure.
The supplied compensation-to-receipts input exceeds one after vintage and harmonization adjustments, so translating task exposure into economic opportunity warrants extra caution.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit time-and-motion study that links payroll to caregiver travel, direct care, documentation, coordination, supervision, recruiting, billing, and administration would materially change the range.
a.rationale: The industry's dominant work consists of bathing, dressing, housekeeping, meal preparation, transportation, companionship, observation, and other direct assistance that current AI cannot physically deliver. AI exposure is therefore a minority of wage-weighted work, concentrated in scheduling, recruiting, documentation, care-plan summaries, customer communications, billing, compliance checks, and management support.

d5.bridge: BLS projects employment of home health and personal care aides across all industries to grow 17% from 2024 to 2034 and attributes demand to a rising older population and the shift from institutional to home- and community-based settings. I convert the ten-year employment projection into an approximately five-year service-demand signal, tempering it because employment can reflect labor intensity, shortages, reimbursement, and occupational demand outside NAICS 624120. The occupational projection may overstate service growth if worker supply or public budgets bind, but may understate latent demand when unmet need and waiting lists suppress observed employment.
caveats: BLS projects occupation employment across multiple industries, not constant-price output for NAICS 624120.
Employment growth may understate unmet demand while overstating output growth if staffing per client rises.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year six-digit real revenue, authorized-hour, delivered-hour, waitlist, and private-pay utilization data by service type and payer would materially change the estimate.
d5.rationale: Aging, disability prevalence, preference for community living, and policy movement toward HCBS support real demand growth. Growth is constrained by affordability, Medicaid budgets and eligibility, unpaid family care, worker shortages, and the possibility that authorized services cannot be staffed, so realized constant-quality demand should grow more slowly than unconstrained need.

caveats: No direct denominator of firms satisfying all size, independence, recurrence, and transferability criteria was found.
The frozen firm count is itself margin-bridged with an estimated margin for a thin-margin, publicly funded industry.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census linking six-digit classification, for-profit status, independent ownership, payer mix, recurring service authorizations, normalized EBITDA, and customer concentration would materially change the estimate.
e.rationale: Many LMM agencies provide inherently recurring outsourced home-care, homemaker, day-program, or disability-support services and therefore fit the lens. Eligibility is reduced by grant-dependent or nonprofit programs without transferable commercial economics, organizations dominated by public or affiliated-party arrangements, highly owner-dependent firms, and providers whose normalized EBITDA does not remain in the target band despite revenue scale.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 624120 ($67,897,404,691) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 624120 ($71,865,755,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0800 (margins.json[624]: ESTIMATE: social assistance runs thin, largely grant/Medicaid-funded; 8% placeholder chosen before deriving results); revenue/employee $53,882 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 624120); class <5: 15,519 firms, avg revenue/firm $134,508 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $10,761 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 3,777 firms, avg revenue/firm $501,503 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $40,120 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 3,787 firms, avg revenue/firm $922,455 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $73,796 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 7,792 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,179,543 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $174,363 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 2,744 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,188,986 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $735,119 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 724 firms, avg revenue/firm $30,653,651 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,452,292 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 724

o.bridge: BLS describes home health and personal care aides as assisting with bathing, dressing, housekeeping, meals, transportation, social engagement, basic observation, and physically demanding movement or support. Medicaid describes HCBS as services delivered in a beneficiary's home or community instead of an institution. I map those embodied, relational, and accountable duties to the share of year-five formal service still requiring an agency or comparable accountable operator. This may overstate agency necessity where consumer-directed care, unpaid family care, remote monitoring, or self-service substitutes for formal providers, but may understate it because vulnerable clients and payers require supervision, documentation, backup staffing, and incident accountability.
caveats: The occupation source combines home health aides with personal care aides and spans multiple industries.
The estimate distinguishes substitution for the agency model from software assistance within an agency-delivered service.
o.change_evidence: Longitudinal payer data comparing agency-directed, consumer-directed, unpaid, remote-monitoring, and fully self-served hours by client acuity would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Software can assist coordination, reminders, documentation, monitoring, and family communication but cannot replace most hands-on activities of daily living, safe transfers, in-person observation, transportation, companionship, or responsibility for service continuity. Consumer direction and technology can bypass an agency for a minority of hours, yet most paid formal care remains operator-requiring over five years.

q.bridge: MACPAC reports that Medicaid financed 60.3% of the broad 2023 category of other health, residential, and personal care that includes many HCBS. CMS's Access Rule generally requires, beginning after its phase-in, that at least 80% of Medicaid payments for homemaker, home health aide, and personal care services be spent on direct-care-worker compensation, with exceptions and small-provider flexibility. I use the payer share as an indicator of administered pricing and the compensation rule as a constraint on retaining implemented benefits as profit. The broad spending category may overstate Medicaid exposure for private-pay elder care and understate it for disability services; the 80% rule directly applies only to specified Medicaid HCBS and could exclude some costs, providers, or services.
caveats: The Medicaid payer statistic uses a broader spending category than NAICS 624120.
The federal compensation requirement has exceptions, a future applicability date, and uncertain state implementation.
q.change_evidence: Agency-level cohort data separating Medicaid, managed-care, grant, insurance, and private-pay revenue, then tracing implemented productivity gains into wages, reimbursement changes, client prices, and contribution margin over five years, would materially change the range.
q.rationale: Providers can retain benefits from fewer scheduling gaps, lower overtime, faster recruiting and onboarding, cleaner claims, reduced administrative effort, and improved client retention. Capture is limited where public fee schedules or managed-care contracts fix revenue, renewal rates reflect costs, competition shares gains, or the Medicaid compensation rule directs most payments to direct-care compensation rather than overhead or profit. Private-pay agencies with differentiated service and local scarcity offer more upside.

rho.bridge: Census's cross-industry 2026 AI supplement reports 18% firm use, 32% employment-weighted use, use in three or fewer functions by 57% of adopters, and worker-task use at 23% of firms. I project these broad adoption and depth measures five years forward for the exposed tasks in elder and disability services. The broad sample likely overstates near-term adoption by thin-margin, fragmented care providers, while severe staffing pressure and the spread of AI within scheduling, electronic visit verification, payroll, and case-management platforms may make it understate eventual implementation.
caveats: The adoption source covers all U.S. businesses, not NAICS 624120.
The five-year value is a projection and depends on state-specific privacy, labor, and Medicaid rules.
rho.change_evidence: Provider-level surveys or software telemetry measuring production use of AI by workflow, error rates, human review, claim acceptance, and hours saved across Medicaid and private-pay agencies would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Implementation should be meaningful in caregiver matching, schedule optimization, recruiting, multilingual communications, documentation drafting, billing edits, service-plan review, and exception detection. Privacy, Medicaid and state compliance, client vulnerability, fragmented software, limited investment capacity, and the need for human review constrain deployment and prevent full implementation.

s5.bridge: Gallup reports that 22% of U.S. employer-business owners planned a sale or transfer within five years. BizBuySell documents completed sales and stable valuation benchmarks for home health care businesses from 2021 through 2025, an adjacent category that explicitly includes medical and nonmedical home services. I discount the broad intention rate to qualifying control transfers because plans may not close and because gifts, internal transfers, subscale agencies, nonprofit changes, and regulated license or contract transitions may not qualify. Gallup's owner-intention sample may overstate realized closings, while marketplace data omit private transactions and may understate deal flow.
caveats: Gallup covers all employer businesses and measures intentions rather than completed transfers.
BizBuySell's home health category mixes medical providers outside NAICS 624120 with nonmedical home-care agencies and is a voluntary marketplace sample.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year cohort of independent NAICS 624120 firms with owner age, sale attempts, closed control transfers, buyer type, payer approvals, and failed-deal reasons would materially change the probability.
s5.rationale: A demonstrated market for home-care agencies and broad owner succession pressure support material transfer availability. Realized qualifying transfers remain lower than intentions because reimbursement exposure, labor shortages, license or payer enrollment, client concentration, and quality or compliance diligence can prevent or delay transactions.

principal_driver: The principal driver is recurring, demographically supported demand combined with labor scarcity that makes better scheduling, recruiting, documentation, and claims operations economically valuable.
principal_weakness: The principal weakness is that embodied human care remains nonautomatable while administered payment and direct-care compensation requirements sharply limit retention of productivity gains.

run_meta.title: Services for the Elderly and Persons with Disabilities

sources.supports: Defines the industry as nonresidential social assistance including day care, nonmedical home care or homemaker services, social activities, group support, and companionship, with residential, vocational, and in-home health care classified elsewhere.
sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 624120 Services for the Elderly and Persons with Disabilities

sources.supports: Reports 2,276,120 jobs and the six-digit occupational mix, including 84.23% healthcare support, 82.28% home health and personal care aides, 3.65% community and social service, 2.75% office support, 1.77% management, and 0.96% business and financial operations.
sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 624120

sources.supports: Describes direct personal, household, transportation, social, observational, documentation, and physical duties and projects employment growth of 17% from 2024 to 2034, citing population aging and the shift to home- and community-based care.
sources.title: Occupational Outlook Handbook: Home Health and Personal Care Aides

sources.supports: Reports nationally representative late-2025 firm AI use, employment-weighted use, functional breadth, worker-task adoption, leading generative-AI tasks, augmentation, and rare AI-related employment decreases.
sources.title: The Microstructure of AI Diffusion: Evidence from Firms, Business Functions, and Worker Tasks

sources.supports: Describes HCBS oversight, reporting and payment transparency, and the phased requirement generally directing at least 80% of specified Medicaid homemaker, home health aide, and personal care payments to direct-care-worker compensation.
sources.title: Ensuring Access to Medicaid Services Final Rule (CMS-2442-F)

sources.supports: Reports that Medicaid financed 60.3% of the broad other health, residential, and personal care category in 2023, a category that includes a variety of home- and community-based services.
sources.title: MACPAC: Medicaid Spending

sources.supports: Reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned a sale or transfer within five years and documents the survey population and differences between employer and nonemployer owners.
sources.title: Most U.S. Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

sources.supports: Documents completed home health care business sales and valuation, revenue, earnings, and time-on-market benchmarks from 2021 through 2025, including nonmedical home-service agencies within the adjacent category.
sources.title: Home Health Care Business Valuation Benchmarks

title: Services for the Elderly and Persons with Disabilities
